moment_text: I would say that, you know, I always knew I wanted to go into healthcare of some, mostly primary health is what I thought I was gonna be doing. I come from a long legacy of primary healthcare providers and so I kind of just took on that identity and assumed that was my path. And then I got into college and I was. The pre-med path, taking my biology classes and, and I just happened to take a psychology class and I really enjoyed it in such a natural way. So then I took another, 'cause I had two social science requirements and I love that one even more. And then I took an upper level psych class. It was abnormal psychology taught by an amazing woman who became my professional and personal mentor later on. And I was like, that's it. I'm, I'm done. I love this. I love this. Entire subject matter. It was just naturally intriguing and, and just pulling me in and I knew it was my calling. So I decided to change my major and that was a, that was a big deal because my dad was very invested in my pre-med career. I told him I was changing. He was extremely disappointed and I, I kid you not told me that this is his worst nightmare. And I was going into a soft science because it was easy. And I didn't care because I loved it. I knew this was right for me. So I stuck to my path and, and my choice and I have not looked back since.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible because it contains clear barrier signals (lack of family support, stigma around 'soft science') and agency signals (making a major life decision despite disapproval) within a family system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is a clinical psychologist, so her role is tagged as 'educator' as she is educating the audience through her professional journey.

moment_text: I have been so happy. And I will say I went, I went on, I got my master's degree first in clinical psych, knowing that the clinical psychology is a non terminal degree. I was nurturing that as well. And then I went back for my PhD and that was kind of an interesting journey too. I've shared this with lots of young women that I've mentored because. Even within the field of psychology, my career has taken several turns. And so when I first got in my PhD program, you know, clinical psychology, the model for that is a scientist practitioner model. So we need to be scientists first and foremost, and then use that science center clinical practice. But the way it kind of gets translated sometimes to these programs. You need to be a scientist. You need to do research or you're a sellout, or at least that's how I interpreted it. So I quickly adopted that belief system and felt like I knew I wanted to teach and go into academia, and so I needed to be a researcher.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is eligible as it details a barrier (stigma against non-research paths, a training gap in how the scientist-practitioner model is presented) within the medical training system, leading to an agency response of enduring this pressure.

moment_text: So I told myself I love research and convinced myself I did, and I do value research. But what I found out was I actually don't love doing it. I did a lot of it. And they had, you know, they had, um, oncology, radiology, diagnostic imaging, pathology, all these brilliant minds sitting around the table so vested in these s and I was so jealous. moment_text: And so that was another time when my gut and my instincts were telling me, Kara, you know, maybe you need to take a different path. And so I did. But, you know, he was coming from a man's perspective of the wife at home supporting him and feeling like he could have it all. I don't wanna peak, I wanna keep evolving as long as I'm here. And so, you know, being able to do that with adults as well can. Yeah, it, it, the kiddos are fun 'cause they just absorb it up. You know, they're just like, if they absorb it, they absorb it. Adults sometimes can be a little bit, um, more skeptical, like it's not gonna work. You know, they've had a lot of experiences that have been hard where maybe they haven't had the successes they want, but I think once they can experience that success and feel these meaningful shifts, even if they're small in the beginning, um, you know, it can have an impact there as well.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains an emotional signal (resignation) and a barrier signal (lack_of_validation from past experiences) within a healthcare provider interaction context.
notes_for_reviewer: This moment contrasts the experience of treating adults versus children, noting adult skepticism as a barrier born from past negative experiences.

moment_text: It, it you, you can't ever coast really. You have to constantly, you be intentional about, you know, what these, these different processes you're trying to, to put in place and be consistent in their use, and the moment that you stop it starts to creep back. I, I just had that where all of a sudden I was getting worked up and I, I could tell I was just spiking in, in my reactions. Like in a way that I normally don't, I was like, oh, okay. I need to, I gotta, I gotta pull back a little bit and like readjust. And, and I, I could do it. I could do it. I just gotta put a little more effort into it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong agency signals (tracking_symptoms, making_decisions) demonstrating a personal, lived experience of self-regulation.

moment_text: I don't feel dire about the state of people and, and I, I don't feel like we are in a, a horrible place and that, that I feel like sometimes gets projected a little bit. I think that there are unique challenges we have because of our culture. I think in some ways other things have gotten better. I think the fact that that psychology and. Mental wellness has is such a, a more common conversation and people are more open to it is very encouraging to me and so. I am a little bit of an an optimistic person, so there might be other providers that would have a different opinion, but I feel like things are moving in the right direction, and I hope that people can be open to bringing these practices, their daily life so that the experiences they have, their life lived experiences are in line with what they want to get during their lifetime. And we could do that.

moment_text: I really think that the, the message I, I want to send and I've tried to send in my practice is getting back to what I said about my own life journey. It's, it's really focusing on who you authentically are. Like knowing who you are and being true to that. Not trying to be something else, um, that's just not really who you are. And, and, and hold onto it and let that guide you in your decisions in life. And I feel like that. Is such an important thing to do, and we'll have so much more fulfillment from that process.

moment_text: I love that. Know who you are and being true to your authentic self. I just absolutely love that. Um, this has been an incredible conversation. Thank you so much for getting under the hood with me and for sharing your life story.
